metadata.focus: Global prime movers — specialized gap-filling categories (heads of state, billionaires, and top executives tracked separately)
metadata.methodology: Three-stage LLM Council (Karpathy method): Stage 1 - Independent responses, Stage 2 - Peer review, Stage 3 - Chairman synthesis by Claude Opus 4.6

claude.name: Yuval Noah Harari
claude.role: Israel / Historian and public intellectual, Hebrew University of Jerusalem
claude.justification: His frameworks on AI, dataism, and the future of humanity directly shape discourse among world leaders, tech regulators, and international forums like the WEF.

claude.name: Jeffrey Sachs
claude.role: USA / Economist, Columbia University
claude.justification: Remains a leading voice on sustainable development, global poverty, and geopolitical diplomacy, advising UN bodies and governments across the Global South while shaping debate on US foreign policy.

claude.name: Joseph Stiglitz
claude.role: USA / Economist, Columbia University
claude.justification: His critiques of neoliberalism, globalization, and inequality continue to influence IMF reform debates, progressive fiscal policy worldwide, and Global South economic strategies.

claude.name: Daron Acemoglu
claude.role: USA (Turkey-born) / Economist, MIT; 2024 Nobel laureate
claude.justification: His Nobel-winning work on institutions and prosperity directly informs World Bank governance frameworks, development policy, and debates on AI and labor markets.

claude.name: Thomas Piketty
claude.role: France / Economist, Paris School of Economics
claude.justification: His research on wealth inequality and advocacy for global wealth taxes actively shapes EU fiscal debates, OECD tax policy, and progressive movements worldwide.

claude.name: Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala
claude.role: Nigeria / Director-General, World Trade Organization
claude.justification: As WTO head she directly shapes global trade rules, vaccine equity frameworks, and dispute resolution affecting every major economy.

claude.name: Esther Duflo
claude.role: France-USA / Economist, MIT; Nobel laureate
claude.justification: Her randomized-control-trial approach to poverty alleviation has become the dominant methodology at major development agencies and governments across Africa and South Asia.

claude.name: Noam Chomsky
claude.role: USA / Linguist and political intellectual, University of Arizona
claude.justification: Despite advanced age, his critiques of US foreign policy, media, and militarism remain widely cited and influential across global anti-war, anti-imperialist, and progressive movements.

claude.name: Raj Chetty
claude.role: USA / Economist, Harvard University
claude.justification: His big-data research on economic mobility directly shapes US federal and state education, housing, and tax policy and is increasingly adopted internationally.

claude.name: Mariana Mazzucato
claude.role: Italy-UK / Economist, University College London
claude.justification: Her 'entrepreneurial state' and mission-oriented innovation frameworks are actively adopted by the EU, WHO, and multiple national industrial strategies.

claude.name: Yoshua Bengio
claude.role: Canada / Computer scientist, Université de Montréal; Turing Award laureate
claude.justification: A leading voice on AI safety and governance, he directly advises the UN, G7, and multiple governments on AI regulation frameworks.

claude.name: Francis Fukuyama
claude.role: USA / Political scientist, Stanford University
claude.justification: His evolving analyses of liberalism, identity politics, and democratic decay remain central reference points for policymakers grappling with democratic backsliding worldwide.

claude.name: Katharine Hayhoe
claude.role: USA-Canada / Climate scientist, Texas Tech University
claude.justification: One of the most effective science communicators bridging climate research to policy and public engagement, influencing US climate discourse and faith-community mobilization.

claude.name: Branko Milanovic
claude.role: USA (Serbia-born) / Economist, CUNY and Luxembourg Income Study
claude.justification: His 'elephant curve' and global inequality research frame how international institutions and media understand globalization's winners and losers.

claude.name: Amartya Sen
claude.role: India-UK-USA / Economist and philosopher, Harvard University; Nobel laureate
claude.justification: His capabilities approach remains embedded in the UN Human Development Index and actively informs global debates on justice, famine prevention, and democratic values.

claude.name: Jürgen Habermas
claude.role: Germany / Philosopher, Goethe University Frankfurt (emeritus)
claude.justification: His theories of the public sphere, deliberative democracy, and European integration continue to shape EU constitutional debates and global democratic theory.

claude.name: Jason Hickel
claude.role: UK (Eswatini-born) / Economic anthropologist, Institute for Environmental Science and Technology, Barcelona
claude.justification: A leading proponent of degrowth whose work increasingly influences EU Green Deal debates, UN sustainability discussions, and Global South advocacy.

claude.name: Shoshana Zuboff
claude.role: USA / Social scientist, Harvard Business School (emerita)
claude.justification: Her 'surveillance capitalism' framework directly shaped the EU's Digital Services Act, global data privacy regulation, and tech accountability movements.

claude.name: Stuart Russell
claude.role: UK-USA / Computer scientist, UC Berkeley
claude.justification: His work on AI alignment and existential risk from advanced AI actively informs the UN AI advisory body, US executive orders, and global AI governance debates.

claude.name: Jayati Ghosh
claude.role: India / Economist, University of Massachusetts Amherst

gemini.name: Yuval Noah Harari

gemini.name: Francis Fukuyama

gemini.name: Esther Duflo
gemini.justification: Her experimental approach to alleviating poverty dictates the funding strategies of the World Bank and major global NGOs, directly impacting millions of lives in developing economies.

gemini.name: Vaclav Smil

gemini.name: Nouriel Roubini
gemini.justification: His framing of 'megathreats'—specifically the intersection of debt crises and geopolitical instability—guides risk management for central banks and sovereign wealth funds.

gemini.name: Shoshana Zuboff

gemini.name: Jeffrey Sachs

gemini.name: Timothy Snyder

gemini.name: Cass Sunstein

gemini.name: Peter Singer
gemini.justification: His utilitarian ethics continue to drive the Effective Altruism movement, directing billions of dollars in philanthropic capital toward specific global health and animal welfare interventions.

gemini.name: Kimberlé Crenshaw
gemini.justification: Her development of Critical Race Theory and intersectionality fundamentally shapes the DEI (Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion) policies of multinational corporations and educational institutions globally.

rankings.reasoning: Best balance of global scope, specificity in justifications, and adherence to the category. Includes non-Western and interdisciplinary figures (Rockström, Hinton), provides concrete mechanisms of influence (e.g., Mazzucato's adoption by EU and Latin American governments, Rockström's planetary boundaries as basis for UN negotiations), and reflects current developments like Acemoglu's 2024 Nobel and Hinton's AI safety advocacy. Justifications are detailed and tied to specific institutional impacts, though some claims (e.g., Harari as 'primary philosophical baseline' for WEF/UN/G20) are somewhat overstated.

Strong category adherence with well-chosen academics and detailed policy-impact justifications. Includes Duflo, Banerjee, and Mazzucato with specific mechanisms of influence. However, including Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala is questionable since she is primarily a policymaker/WTO Director-General rather than an academic or public intellectual. Lacks some breadth in disciplines (heavy economics focus) and misses key figures like Harari and Hinton who are shaping major public debates. Justifications are precise but slightly less current-event-anchored than Model C.

Reasonable selections but justifications are more generic and less specific about mechanisms of influence. Includes solid choices like Krugman, Stiglitz, Piketty, and Acemoglu, but the reasoning reads as conventional and less tied to current (2025-2026) developments. The list appears more Western-centric and economics-heavy. While Harari at #1 is defensible, the overall justifications lack the granularity and specificity of the other two models in explaining how these figures are currently shaping policy and discourse.

Most accurate and category-consistent: avoids major factual overclaims, includes a clear public-intellectual (Krugman) alongside widely recognized global economists (Stiglitz, Piketty, Acemoglu) and Harari; justifications are plausible and not overly specific; overall reads like a defensible top-tier shortlist for 2026.

Generally strong and within-category, with credible figures and reasonable influence pathways (UN, IMF/World Bank debates, EU/OECD tax discourse). However, it contains a notable accuracy issue (Acemoglu described as “Nobel-winning”) and some claims are a bit overstated/specific (e.g., direct shaping of particular institutional frameworks) without support; global scope is decent but less clearly broad than it implies.

Includes several appropriate academics/public intellectuals (Mazzucato, Hinton, Rockström, Acemoglu, Harari) and better non-economics breadth, but it is the least accurate and most hyperbolic: claims like Harari as the “primary philosophical baseline” for WEF/UN/G20 and Rockström as the “operating system” for UN negotiations are exaggerated; it also repeats the major factual error by calling Acemoglu a “2024 Nobel laureate,” undermining reliability despite broader topical scope.

Strongest on current relevance (AI safety, planetary boundaries, 2024 Nobel), precise justifications tied to WEF/UN/G20/EU, pure academics/intellectuals, broad global scope across tech/climate/policy.
Accurate economists/intellectuals with solid global policy links (WEF/UN/IMF/World Bank), good reasoning, adheres well to category, comprehensive but less diverse fields.
Good development focus but Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala stretches 'academics/intellectuals' as WTO executive; reasoning solid but less current edge and global breadth vs. others.

This response demonstrates the strongest adherence to the category, listing figures who are primarily defined by their academic and intellectual output rather than holding executive power. It correctly contextualizes Daron Acemoglu as a Nobel winner (2024) and highlights Jeffrey Sachs' high relevance in the 2026 geopolitical climate. The justifications are specific, linking ideas to concrete policy bodies (WEF, UN).

Model B includes Mariana Mazzucato, whose 'mission economy' frameworks are highly influential in 2026 EU/UN policy, showing deep domain knowledge. However, it ranks lower than A because it includes Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala, who, as Director-General of the WTO, fits the 'Global Leader/Policymaker' category better than 'Academic/Public Intellectual'. The reasoning is otherwise excellent.

While accurate, this response relies on more generic justifications ('books and talks', 'columns') and feels slightly dated. The inclusion of Paul Krugman suggests a focus on US-centric media influence, which is arguably less globally dominant in 2026 compared to the structural reform agendas proposed by figures like Mazzucato or Sachs.

rankings.reasoning: Best global scope by including non-Western figures like Shen Haixiong and cultural figures like Joe Rogan alongside traditional media leaders. Strong justifications that explain *why* each figure matters (e.g., 'narrative baseline for Western policymakers'). Covers wire services, state media, new media, and legacy outlets comprehensively.

Solid institutional coverage with accurate picks like Tim Davie, Mostefa Souag, Alessandra Galloni, and Daisy Veerasingham. Good global breadth including Arabic-language media. Justifications are clear but slightly more generic than Model C's. Slightly less innovative in recognizing new media and non-Western state media influence.

Weakest global scope—top picks like Katherine Maher (NPR), Ayman Mohyeldin, and Rania Khalek are heavily U.S.-centric and lack the structural global influence of wire service heads, major state broadcasters, or the BBC. Rania Khalek's inclusion as a top-5 global media figure overstates her influence relative to institutional leaders. Misses major non-Western media figures entirely in the visible top 5.

Best overall on Feb 2026 accuracy and category fit: it centers institutional media power (BBC, major wire services, NYT) with defensible claims about global reach and agenda-setting. The selections are broadly plausible, globally oriented, and the rationales align with how influence propagates (syndication, distribution, editorial control).

Strong reasoning and global scope by mixing Western agenda-setters (NYT/Reuters/BBC) with major non-Western state media (China’s CGTN/CCTV apparatus) and a major podcast figure. However, it’s narrower on cultural breadth (leans news/politics + one podcaster) and some superlatives (e.g., “most cited newspaper,” “largest multimedia news provider”) are more contestable than Model B’s framing.

Weakest on current-information accuracy and rigor: it contains likely incorrect/dated leadership attributions (e.g., Deborah Turness described as heading the world’s largest news organization; Mark Thompson described as leading CNN) and overstates individual anchor primacy (Amanpour). It also overweights Al Jazeera management details and provides less globally comprehensive, institutionally grounded justification than B or C.

Highest accuracy with current roles (e.g., Reuters, BBC DG, China Media Group head); superior reasoning on global narrative scale (1.4B audience, citations); best category fit including cultural (Rogan); truly global (China, wires, BBC).
Accurate roles mostly, strong justifications on narrative framing; good media focus with Al Jazeera for ME/Global South; solid but less comprehensive global scope than C.
Accurate but US/UK-heavy (NPR, WSJ, MSNBC); weaker reasoning for niche figures like Khalek; limited global scope and less comprehensive.

Best demonstrates 'Global Scope' and 'Influence' by identifying the leaders of the structural pillars of global news (AP, Reuters, BBC, Al Jazeera). The focus on agency heads and public broadcasters correctly identifies where global narratives originate.

Lists real individuals with accurate roles, but the selection is heavily US-centric (NPR, MSNBC) and the ranking logic is flawed, elevating niche alternative media figures and domestic correspondents to the same tier as global editors-in-chief.

While the first four entries are strong selections of high-power executives, the fifth entry ('Tala Al-Rashed') appears to be a hallucination or factual error regarding the leadership of Al Jazeera, which violates the primary criterion of accuracy.

rankings.reasoning: Strongest justifications with specific mechanisms of influence (conditionality, norm-setting, agenda-setting). Accurate current information with Guterres appropriately at #1 given his convening role. Well-reasoned explanations linking each leader to concrete policy levers. Good adherence to the category with focus on IGOs, and likely strong coverage across the full 20.

Adds useful temporal context (e.g., 'final year of tenure,' 'assumed leadership in late 2024') that demonstrates awareness of current dynamics. However, including Mark Rutte/NATO is a borderline category fit since NATO is a military alliance rather than a traditional IGO/think tank. The contextual framing is a strength but the category adherence is slightly weaker.

Accurate and competent but omits António Guterres from the top 5, which is a significant oversight given the UN Secretary-General's centrality to this category. Justifications are adequate but more generic (e.g., 'primary health authority,' 'largest multilateral development bank') with less specificity about mechanisms of influence compared to Model A. Quantitative details like '$28 trillion' and '$70B+' are helpful but don't fully compensate.

Best fit to the category and global scope: it centers IGOs and major global foundations (WHO, World Bank, IMF, WTO, Gates) with broadly accurate, durable role-based justifications. It avoids obvious category errors and its claims are generally time-robust for Feb 2026.

Strong on IGOs/foundation leadership and generally accurate, but it contains a notable factual issue: Mark Suzman does not “direct” the Gates Foundation (he is CEO, not the foundation’s top governing authority), and the “$8B annually” figure is debatable/variable. Otherwise the reasoning is solid and category adherence is good.

Weaker category adherence and accuracy: it includes NATO leadership (Mark Rutte) which is an intergovernmental military alliance and sits awkwardly outside an NGOs/foundations/think-tanks framing; it also asserts a specific late-2024 leadership change that is not reliably accurate as of Feb 2026. Overall less aligned with the category and less comprehensive beyond a Western-security lens.
Excellent category adherence with core IGOs (UN, IMF, WB, WHO, WTO); strong global scope, comprehensive coverage, solid policy-focused reasoning; accurate for 2026.
Strong fit (WB, IMF, WHO, Gates Foundation, UN) with quantifiable justifications; good global comprehensiveness and accuracy, includes foundation diversity early.
NATO (Rutte) fits IGO technically but less global/development-focused; omits key figures like WHO/WTO in top 5, reducing comprehensiveness; reasoning good but category stretch.

Model A provides the most sophisticated assessment of influence for the February 2026 timeframe. It correctly identifies the rising dominance of financial institutions (World Bank, IMF) and large private foundations (Gates) over political bodies like the UN, given Guterres is in his final 'lame duck' year. It also offers the best diversity within the top 5, representing both IGOs and Global Foundations effectively.

Model B provides a highly accurate, traditional list of the 'Big 5' IGO leaders. While factually sound, it focuses exclusively on IGOs in the top 5, missing the 'Foundations' or 'NGOs' aspect of the prompt's category compared to Model A. Additionally, ranking the UN Secretary-General as #1 in 2026 ignores the geopolitical paralysis that often limits that role compared to the heads of financial bodies.

Model C is the weakest due to its ranking logic. Placing the WHO Director-General as the #1 most influential global figure in 2026 (post-pandemic phase) is unrealistic compared to the leaders of the global economy (IMF/World Bank) or the UN. Additionally, the description of the World Bank 'channeling trillions' is slightly hyperbolic regarding direct operational control.

rankings.reasoning: Best adherence to the 'shadow power' concept by focusing on figures who operate behind formal structures—Sheikh Tahnoon, Erik Prince, Jared Kushner, and Peter Thiel are quintessential shadow operators who wield enormous influence without holding traditional government office. The justifications are specific and well-reasoned, citing concrete mechanisms of influence (sovereign wealth, private military networks, defense tech, unofficial diplomacy). Broadest global scope spanning Gulf, Russia, US, Africa, and tech/defense sectors.

Strong analytical framework and good identification of figures like Wang Qishan and Patrushev as system operators. However, leading with MBZ and MBS—who are heads of state—somewhat undermines the 'shadow power' framing, even though the response acknowledges this tension. The reasoning is sophisticated but the category fit is weaker for several top entries. Good global scope covering Russia, China, and the Gulf.

Heavily skewed toward Russian oligarchs and Kremlin-adjacent figures (Sechin, Kovalchuk, Timchenko, Rotenberg, Dmitriev in just the top 5), which limits global comprehensiveness. While these are legitimate shadow power figures, the list reads more like a sanctions target roster than a globally diverse ranking. The justifications are adequate but less nuanced than the other models, and the narrow geographic focus weakens the 'global' dimension of the category.

Most internally consistent and current-leaning for Feb 2026: Sechin/Kovalchuk/Timchenko/Rotenberg/Dmitriev are plausible “operator” nodes for energy, finance, sanctions workarounds and deal-brokering. Justifications are generally aligned with shadow-power mechanisms (commodities, capital flows, patronage networks). While still Russia-heavy, it reads like a coherent operator set rather than speculative succession claims.

Strong on Kovalchuk/Patrushev/Timchenko and the behind-the-scenes framing, but it leans on shakier or more speculative assertions (e.g., presenting Andrey Troshev as the clear, singular manager of the post-Prigozhin PMC/resource network). Also narrower in scope (heavily Russia-centric) and the “MBZ’s circle” entry is less cleanly specified than Model C’s direct identification of Tahnoon.

Best global breadth in concept (Gulf finance/AI + US private-sector power + PMCs), but several claims are overstated or weakly supported for Feb 2026 (e.g., Erik Prince “massive” control over DRC cobalt operations; Kushner as a major policy-shaping conduit outside formal channels; Thiel as directly shaping Western security policy via Palantir). The reasoning often inflates influence and blurs “shadow operator” vs. prominent public figure, reducing accuracy and category adherence.
Most accurate and comprehensive global scope with true shadow players (private sector US, UAE, Russia, Africa); excellent reasoning on covert networks; perfect category fit.
Strong accuracy on Russian/UAE insiders; good justifications and shadow adherence; somewhat Russia-heavy, limiting global breadth.
Includes public heads of state (MBZ, MBS), poor category adherence; solid reasoning but narrower scope and less shadow focus.

This response best demonstrates the 'shadow power' definition by selecting specific operators (Sheikh Tahnoun, Troshev) rather than heads of state. It shows strong temporal awareness for the 2026 context (identifying Prigozhin's successor) and balances Russian influence with critical Middle Eastern operators.

While the global scope is good, the inclusion of heads of state (MBZ, MBS) weakens the adherence to the 'shadow/operator' category. Additionally, listing Wang Qishan—who largely retired from active power years prior—suggests outdated reasoning for a 2026 timeline.

This response fails the 'global scope' criterion by listing five Russians with no geographic diversity. Furthermore, it contains a significant factual error regarding Gennady Timchenko, claiming he still oversees Gunvor's trades, a stake he divested over a decade ago.

rankings.reasoning: Most accurate and category-faithful: focuses on central bankers (squarely within scope), avoids obvious misidentifications, and provides solid, globally relevant monetary-policy justifications across major economies (US, eurozone, China, Japan, UK). While it may underweight non-central-bank senior officials/military, what it includes is broadly correct and globally scoped.

Mixed accuracy and weaker category adherence: Powell/Lagarde/von der Leyen fit well, but naming Marco Rubio as “America’s top diplomat” and Pete Hegseth as running DoD is highly likely inaccurate for Feb 2026, undermining the list’s current-information reliability. Reasoning is generally plausible, but the apparent role errors and US-centric tilt reduce overall quality and global comprehensiveness.

Contains major current-information errors and questionable specifics: Gen. Charles Q. Brown Jr. is unlikely to be serving a fixed term “until 2027” as the highest-ranking U.S. military officer by Feb 2026, and several term/mandate claims are presented with unwarranted precision. Cai Qi is a senior official, but the list leans on opaque internal-party power assertions rather than verifiable role-based influence, weakening reasoning and accuracy despite decent global scope.
Perfect accuracy with verified term lengths into 2026/27, excellent global scope (China #2, EU, US military), precise justifications, ideal category adherence with true military chief.
Accurate Trump admin positions (Rubio, Hegseth confirmed by Feb 2026), strong reasoning on influence, good category fit, but US-heavy top 5 limits global comprehensiveness.
Superior reasoning detail and global scope (China, Russia high), solid category adherence, but critical accuracy failure with Yellen no longer Treasury Secretary.

Demonstrates superior temporal accuracy by correctly projecting the US political landscape in February 2026 (Trump 2.0 administration), identifying likely officials like Marco Rubio and Pete Hegseth. It also balances the category mix well between central bankers, EU officials, and military/diplomatic chiefs.

Maintains high factual accuracy regarding central bank tenures which extend into 2026. However, it ranks lower than A because its top 5 is exclusively focused on central bankers, lacking the necessary variety of 'Government Officials' and 'Military Chiefs' requested in the category.

Fails significantly on the first criterion of accuracy. By listing Janet Yellen as a key figure in February 2026, it ignores the outcome of the 2024 US election and the resulting government transition, rendering the list anachronistic despite decent reasoning.

stage_3_final_list.name: Mark Rutte
stage_3_final_list.role: NATO / Secretary General
stage_3_final_list.justification: As NATO's chief political coordinator, he drives defense spending commitments, strategic consensus, and alliance cohesion among 32 member states facing persistent threats on the eastern flank.
stage_3_final_list.consensus_notes: Listed by Gemini (#10); ChatGPT erroneously listed Stoltenberg, whose term ended in 2024; Rutte is the correct Feb 2026 officeholder managing the alliance's most consequential era.

stage_3_final_list.name: Subrahmanyam Jaishankar
stage_3_final_list.role: India / Minister of External Affairs
stage_3_final_list.justification: Architects India's multi-aligned foreign policy as a rising great power, serving as a critical bridge between the West, Russia, and the Global South while advancing QUAD partnerships.
stage_3_final_list.consensus_notes: Listed by Grok (#18) and Gemini (#15); provides essential Global South representation and reflects India's growing geopolitical weight.

stage_3_final_list.name: Andrei Belousov
stage_3_final_list.role: Russia / Minister of Defense
stage_3_final_list.justification: Manages Russia's wartime military-industrial complex, procurement, and resource allocation sustaining operations in Ukraine, making him central to the conflict's trajectory.
stage_3_final_list.consensus_notes: Listed by Grok (#17) and Gemini (#12); recognized as a key figure in Russia's war economy and military sustainment effort.

stage_3_final_list.name: Elvira Nabiullina
stage_3_final_list.role: Russia / Governor of the Central Bank of Russia
stage_3_final_list.justification: Her technocratic management has been pivotal in shielding the Russian economy from sanctions-induced collapse, enabling the state to sustain prolonged military operations and adapt to financial isolation.
stage_3_final_list.consensus_notes: Listed by Gemini (#9); uniquely important as the central banker keeping a sanctioned major economy operational during wartime, warranting inclusion for her outsized stabilization role.
